question.text: In put-call parity for European options, which formula correctly expresses the relationship?

answer.text: Call - Put = Spot - Present Value of Strike Price (C - P = S - K×e^(-rt))
Call + Put = Spot + Present Value of Strike Price
Call × Put = Spot × Present Value of Strike Price

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Put-call parity states: C - P = S - K×e^(-rt), where C = call premium, P = put premium, S = spot price, K = strike price, r = risk-free rate, t = time to expiry. Any deviation from this creates an arbitrage opportunity. **The Trap:** The relationship is Call MINUS Put, not Call PLUS Put. Also, the strike is discounted (present value), not at face value. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch5 covers put-call parity for equity options. The same principle applies across all European-style options in currency and interest rate markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Put-call parity is the fundamental no-arbitrage condition for European options — if violated, traders can construct synthetic positions to earn risk-free profits.
